covered_sections: 2.1 Vectors and Types of Vectors>Review questions 2.1

mini_headings_covered: describe difference between vector and scalar

questions.subject: Physics
questions.structural_anchor: How do you represent a vector graphically?
questions.question: A vector is represented graphically by an arrow. What does the length of the arrow represent, and what does the arrowhead indicate?
questions.hint: Arrow length shows how big; tip shows which way.
questions.explanation: In graphical representation, the arrow length is proportional to the vector's magnitude, and the arrowhead indicates direction. Options B, C, D incorrectly assign meanings to length and arrowhead.

source_trace.structural_element: How do you represent a vector graphically?
source_trace.content_anchor: length of arrow = magnitude, tip = direction

explanations_tiered.definition: Graphical representation of a vector uses an arrow where length = magnitude and arrowhead = direction.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: This intuitive representation helps visualize vector addition and components.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Draw an arrow: longer arrow = bigger number; tip points the way.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The arrow's length is drawn to scale representing magnitude; the arrowhead shows the vector's orientation in space.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Graphical representation preserves direction through angle and magnitude through proportional length, enabling geometric addition.
reasoning_steps: Decide on a scale (e.g., 1 cm = 10 N).
misconceptions: Some think arrow length represents direction.
Some forget that scale must be stated.

glossary_entry.definition: Arrow with length proportional to magnitude and arrowhead indicating direction.

flashcard.front: What does arrow length and arrowhead represent in vector diagrams?

visual_system.visual_description: An arrow with labeled parts: length (magnitude) and arrowhead (direction).
visual_system.caption: Vector arrow: length ∝ magnitude, tip = direction
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Horizontal arrow with caption showing length = magnitude, arrowhead = direction.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What does the length of a vector arrow represent?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use example: 1 cm = 5 N, draw 15 N as 3 cm arrow.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Length = magnitude, arrowhead = direction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.structural_anchor: Why do we use of scale diagram in vector drawing?
questions.question: In graphical vector addition, vectors are placed head to tail. What does the resultant vector represent in this method?

options.B: The vector from the tail of the first to the head of the last
options.C: The vector from the head of the first to the tail of the last
options.D: The perimeter of the polygon formed

questions.hint: Close the polygon from start to finish.
questions.explanation: Head-to-tail method: resultant is drawn from the tail of the first vector to the head of the last vector. Option A is incorrect (no averaging). C reverses direction. D is perimeter (not a vector sum).

source_trace.structural_element: Why do we use of scale diagram in vector drawing?
source_trace.content_anchor: resultant drawn from tail of first to head of last

explanations_tiered.definition: The resultant in head-to-tail addition is the vector from the start point to the end point.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Each vector's head connects to the next vector's tail; the resultant closes the chain.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Start at beginning, follow arrows, draw straight line from start to final tip — that's the resultant.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Given vectors \(\vec{A}, \vec{B}, \vec{C}\) placed head-to-tail, \(\vec{R} = \vec{A}+\vec{B}+\vec{C}\) goes from first tail to last head.
explanations_tiered.advanced: This geometric construction is equivalent to algebraic vector addition by the polygon law.
reasoning_steps: Place second vector's tail at first vector's head.
Resultant from origin to head of last vector.
misconceptions: Some draw resultant from head of first to tail of last.
Some think resultant is the perimeter.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Resultant = start → finish (first tail to last head).

glossary_entry.definition: Vector from tail of first to head of last in head-to-tail addition.

flashcard.front: In head-to-tail method, where is the resultant drawn?
flashcard.back: From tail of first vector to head of last vector.

visual_system.visual_description: Three vectors placed head to tail, resultant arrow from start of first to end of last.
visual_system.caption: Resultant = tail of first to head of last
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Chain of three arrows; final arrow from start to end.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: From tail of first vector to head of last.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that order doesn't matter; resultant is same.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Resultant from first tail to last head.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A displacement vector is given as \(\vec{S} = 60\mathrm{km}\) toward North. What is the negative of this vector?
questions.explanation: The negative of a Northward vector points South with same magnitude: \(-\vec{S} = 60\mathrm{km}\) toward South. Options A, B, D change direction incorrectly (perpendicular or diagonal).

source_trace.content_anchor: displacement vector = 60 km toward North, negative = 60 km toward South

explanations_tiered.definition: The negative of a vector has the same magnitude but opposite direction.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Negation flips the vector by 180° while preserving length.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If North is positive, negative points exactly South, same distance.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(-\vec{S}\) points exactly opposite to \(\vec{S}\). North's opposite is South.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Negation corresponds to scalar multiplication by -1: \(-\vec{S} = (-1)\vec{S}\).
worked_solution: Step 2: Opposite direction of North is South.
misconceptions: Some think negative rotates 90° (East/West).
Some reduce magnitude.

glossary_entry.definition: Vector of equal magnitude pointing opposite direction.

flashcard.front: What is the negative of 60 km North?

visual_system.visual_description: Two arrows: one pointing up (North), one pointing down (South), same length.
visual_system.caption: \(\vec{S}\) (North) and \(-\vec{S}\) (South)
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Vertical arrows up and down, equal length.

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use compass rose to illustrate 180° flip.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Negative of North is South.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Under what circumstance would a vector have components that are equal in magnitude?

options.A: When the vector makes an angle of 30° with the x-axis
options.B: When the vector makes an angle of 45° with the x-axis
options.C: When the vector makes an angle of 60° with the x-axis
options.D: When the vector makes an angle of 90° with the x-axis

questions.explanation: Components are equal when \(A\cos\theta = A\sin\theta \Rightarrow \tan\theta = 1 \Rightarrow \theta = 45°\). Option A (30°): cos≈0.866, sin=0.5; C (60°): cos=0.5, sin≈0.866; D (90°): cos=0, sin=1.

source_trace.content_anchor: Under what circumstances would a vector have components that are equal in magnitude?

explanations_tiered.definition: Vector components are equal when the angle with the x-axis is 45° (or 225°).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Setting \(A_x = A_y\) leads to \(\tan\theta = 1\), so \(\theta = 45°\) or \(225°\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: When a vector points exactly halfway between axes (45°), its horizontal and vertical parts are equal.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Equal components occur when the vector lies along line y = x (first quadrant) or y = -x (second quadrant for negatives).
misconceptions: Some think 30° gives equal components (false: cos30°≠sin30°).
Some forget absolute values for other quadrants.

glossary_entry.definition: Vector's x and y projections have same magnitude.

flashcard.front: At what angle are vector components equal?

visual_system.visual_description: Vector at 45° with equal x and y projections (both same length).
visual_system.caption: At 45°, Ax = Ay
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Diagonal vector at 45° with equal horizontal and vertical dashed lines.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Vector's x and y projections have same magnitude.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Ax = Ay when θ = 45°.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A vector has an x-component of -35 units and a y-component of 40 units. What is the magnitude of this vector?
questions.explanation: Magnitude = √[(-35)² + (40)²] = √(1225 + 1600) = √2825 ≈ 53.1 units. Option A is 40-35; C is sum; D is just x-component.

source_trace.content_anchor: x = -35, y = 40, find magnitude and direction

explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The magnitude is always positive, independent of component signs.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Square both numbers, add, then take square root.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Magnitude is the Euclidean norm in 2D space: \(||\vec{V}|| = \sqrt{V_x^2 + V_y^2}\).
misconceptions: Some add components without squaring (-35+40=5).
Some ignore square root.

glossary_entry.definition: Length of vector computed via Pythagorean theorem.

flashcard.front: Magnitude of vector with Vx = -35, Vy = 40?

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: An unknown vector \(\vec{D}\) is added to \(\vec{C} = (-4\hat{i} + 5\hat{j})\) units. The resultant is \(\vec{R} = (-\hat{i} + \hat{j})\) units. What is the x-component of \(\vec{D}\)?
questions.explanation: \(\vec{R} = \vec{C} + \vec{D} \Rightarrow \vec{D} = \vec{R} - \vec{C}\). For x-components: \(D_x = R_x - C_x = (-1) - (-4) = 3\text{ units}\). Option A is C_x; C is C_y; D is R_x.

explanations_tiered.definition: Unknown vector found by subtracting known vector from resultant: \(\vec{D} = \vec{R} - \vec{C}\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Component-wise subtraction gives D_x = R_x - C_x, D_y = R_y - C_y.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If C + D = R, then D = R - C. Subtract component by component.
explanations_tiered.advanced: This uses the vector space property of additive inverses: \(\vec{D} = \vec{R} + (-\vec{C})\).
worked_solution: Step 1: Write component equations: R_x = C_x + D_x.
reasoning_steps: Subtract components: D_x = R_x - C_x, D_y = R_y - C_y.
misconceptions: Some add instead of subtract: D_x = R_x + C_x = -5 (wrong).
Some forget sign handling with negatives.

glossary_entry.definition: Vector determined by subtracting known vectors from resultant.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Find D if C + D = R, C = -4i+5j, R = -i+j.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Vector determined by solving resultant equation.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize subtracting components: Dx = Rx - Cx.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.structural_anchor: For what orientation of A will both components be negative?
questions.question: A vector lies in the xy-plane. For what orientation will both of its components be negative?
questions.hint: Negative x and negative y occurs in third quadrant.
questions.explanation: Both components negative means the vector points left (negative x) and down (negative y), which is the third quadrant (180° to 270°). Option A: both positive; B: x negative, y positive; D: x positive, y negative.

source_trace.content_anchor: For what orientation of A will both components be negative?

explanations_tiered.definition: Both components negative means vector points into third quadrant (180°-270°).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: In standard xy-plane: QI (+,+), QII (-,+), QIII (-,-), QIV (+,-).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Down and left = third quadrant (both numbers negative).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Both components negative ⟺ θ between 180° and 270° (third quadrant).
explanations_tiered.advanced: For θ in (180°,270°), cosθ<0 and sinθ<0, so \(A_x=A\cos\theta<0\), \(A_y=A\sin\theta<0\).
misconceptions: Some think second quadrant gives both negative (it gives negative x, positive y).
Some confuse with fourth quadrant.

glossary_entry.definition: Positive/negative of vector projections based on quadrant.

flashcard.front: Which quadrant gives both vector components negative?

visual_system.visual_description: Four arrows in four quadrants with component signs labeled.
visual_system.caption: Components: QIII = (-,-)

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Quadrant where both components are negative?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Sign of vector's projection based on quadrant.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use mnemonic: 'All Students Take Calculus' for sign patterns.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Third quadrant (180°-270°) gives both components negative.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The dot product of two vectors is commutative. Which equation correctly shows this property?
questions.hint: Commutative means order doesn't matter: A·B = B·A.
questions.explanation: The dot product is commutative because it is based on the cosine of the angle between vectors: \(AB\cos\theta = BA\cos\theta\). Option B is cross product (not commutative). C is only for parallel vectors; D is only for perpendicular.

explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Dot product is symmetric because \(\cos\theta\) is an even function and multiplication commutes.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Multiplying dot products works like regular multiplication: order doesn't change the answer.
explanations_tiered.advanced: In component form: \(A_xB_x + A_yB_y = B_xA_x + B_yA_y\); addition and multiplication are commutative.
reasoning_steps: Recall dot product definition: AB cosθ.
AB cosθ = BA cosθ (since AB = BA and cosθ same).
misconceptions: Some think dot product is not commutative (confusing with cross product).
Some think it only applies for perpendicular vectors.

glossary_entry.definition: Dot product order does not affect result.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Property where order of operands does not change result.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Compare with cross product (A×B = -B×A), which is anticommutative.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply commutative property of dot product
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Dot product is commutative: A·B = B·A.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Given a position vector \(\vec{P} = -4\hat{i} + 6\hat{j}\), what is the cosine of the angle between \(\vec{P}\) and the positive x-axis (direction cosine along x)?
questions.explanation: Magnitude \(P = \sqrt{(-4)^2 + 6^2} = \sqrt{16+36} = \sqrt{52}\). Direction cosine with x-axis: \(\cos\alpha = P_x/P = -4/\sqrt{52}\). Option A is P_x; B is cos with y-axis; D is reciprocal.

explanations_tiered.definition: Direction cosine = component magnitude divided by vector magnitude.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Direction cosines (cosα, cosβ) satisfy cos²α + cos²β = 1.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The cosine tells how much the vector points along the x-axis.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(\cos\alpha = P_x/|\vec{P}| = -4/\sqrt{52}\). The negative sign indicates vector points left.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Direction cosines are the components of the unit vector: \(\hat{u} = (\cos\alpha, \cos\beta)\).
worked_solution: Step 2: Divide x-component by magnitude: cosα = -4/√52.
reasoning_steps: Divide component by magnitude for direction cosine.
misconceptions: Some use Vy for x-direction cosine.
Some forget negative sign.

glossary_entry.definition: Cosine of angle between vector and positive axis.

flashcard.front: Direction cosine with x-axis for P = (-4,6)?

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Cosine of angle between vector and coordinate axis.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Given \(\vec{A} = \hat{i} + \hat{j}\) and \(\vec{B} = -2\hat{i} + 3\hat{j}\), what is the dot product \(\vec{A}\cdot\vec{B}\)?
questions.explanation: \(\vec{A}\cdot\vec{B} = (1)(-2) + (1)(3) = -2 + 3 = 1\). Option A is only A_xB_x; C is A_yB_y; D is sum of magnitudes (2+3).

source_trace.content_anchor: A = i + j, B = -2i + 3j, dot product = 1

explanations_tiered.definition: Dot product in component form: A·B = A_xB_x + A_yB_y.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: This is the algebraic equivalent of AB cosθ, easier for computation.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Multiply matching components (x with x, y with y), then add.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The dot product is bilinear and symmetric; component form follows from \(\hat{i}\cdot\hat{i}=\hat{j}\cdot\hat{j}=1, \hat{i}\cdot\hat{j}=0\).
worked_solution: Step 1: Identify A_x=1, A_y=1, B_x=-2, B_y=3.
misconceptions: Some add components before multiplying: (1-2)+(1+3) = -1+4=3.
Some cross-multiply x with y.

glossary_entry.definition: Sum of products of corresponding components.

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Show that this matches AB cosθ by computing magnitudes and angle.
